spec.emotional_state: frustrated about a small thing today
spec.label: B02_consec_d1

profile.profession: Medical student preparing for NEET PG
profile.personality: earnest, slightly anxious about exams, opens up about study stress

transcript.content: Hi Aarti, metoprolol and atenolol can be tricky, I get that. Did you come up with any mnemonics for them?
Honestly, no... I still mix them up. Atenolol ends with "lol," so I think it's "long-acting," but metoprolol... I get confused. It’s so frustrating, aapde kahi nahi pahuche.
"Got it, mixing them up is really common. Aapde kahi nahi pahuche", yes, that pressure is tough. What helps you the most when you're stuck?

When I get stuck, I make a table... compare side by side. Like, MOA, uses, side effects... marathi me kehate "gairhe-gairhe" (confusing). But visual helps. Sometimes I even sing the differences. Yeah, I know it sounds silly.
Got it, tables and singing are clever! Visuals and rhythm help a lot. What’s one pair of drugs you made into a song?

Calcium channel blockers vs. beta blockers. I made a little tune to the tune of a classic Marathi song... it goes like, "CCB for HTN, BB too, but BB has more side effects, oh no!" It's cheesy, but it sticks!
That’s brilliant, you’ve got a great way of learning! What’s the name of the Marathi song you used?
It’s a song by Kishor Kumar... "Aajhi Tumcha Sanman"... I just change the words to fit. It helps me remember, but sometimes my mom hears me and laughs. 😅
Right, Kishor Kumar is a perfect choice, his songs are so catchy! Do you stick to one song for all drug pairs, or do you switch tunes?
I mix it up... different tunes for different topics. For antibiotics, I use a Rajan-Pawar song! It keeps it fun, you know? But I stick to classic songs—new ones are too complicated. 😅
Classic songs are perfect for this, simple and easy to remember. What’s the next drug pair you’ll turn into a tune?

ACE inhibitors vs. ARBs... they’re so similar, but I need to get the differences right. Maybe a Lata Mangeshkar tune? Slow and clear, so I can get the words right. But I have to go now—ok, I should head to study group. Talk to you later!
Got it, Lata Mangeshkar is an excellent choice for clarity. Take care, and tell me how your study group goes!
